text: The surgeon identifies deep endometriosis in the rectovaginal septum and begins the procedure by dissecting and lateralizing the ureter. The peritoneum is opened, and the rectum is isolated on both lateral sides.
The assistant grasps the rectum and applies cranial traction, preparing for central dissection.

The surgeon dissects dense endometriotic adhesions without visible nodules on the rectovaginal septum or Danone-Wheeler's fascia. The posterior vagina and rectal fat are opened and further dissected down towards the anorectal junction.

The surgeon identifies the anorectal junction, revealing the serosal coat over the rectum densely adhered to the posterior vaginal wall. Gentle traction and dissection are performed using bipolar forceps and scissors to separate these structures in the presence of difficult endometriosis.

The surgeon uses bipolar and scissors to dissect the serosal coat, which is densely adhered to the rectovaginal septum. The rectovaginal septum is gradually opened up as the dissection progresses away from fibrotic planes.

The surgeon continues dissection in the caudal direction, approaching the levator ani muscle, which is located laterally to the anorectal junction. This marks the end of the dissection towards the caudal margin.

The surgeon uses bipolar coagulation to gently apply traction on the fibrotic tissue, followed by sharp cuts with scissors to dissect the dense fibrosis. The pararectal fascia is also visibly affected by fibrosis, requiring careful attention to potential bleeding.

The surgeon mobilizes the caudal part of the rectal pillar, coagulating it as part of the procedure. The dissection is completed with the rectum fully mobilized away from the posterior fornix and the posterior vaginal wall.

The surgeon identifies the longitudinal layer and the white anorectal junction. The anterior part of the rectal serosa, forming the posterior part of the Danone-Willers fascia, is dissected and removed from the peritoneal cavity.

The surgeon performs a nerve-sparing procedure, preserving the rectal plexus from the inferior hypogastric nerve, particularly on the right side. The Danone-Willers fascia is being dissected by grasping and gently cutting the serosal covering of the rectum to expose the underlying longitudinal muscle.

The surgeon exposes the longitudinal muscle of the rectum and removes the overlying fat, ensuring the lower one-third of the rectum is completely bare. This involves dissecting and removing the entire Danone-Willers fascia to minimize recurrence risk.

A rectal probe is inserted to check the integrity of the rectum, which is then filled with methylene blue. The dye is removed, confirming the rectum's integrity. The stripped longitudinal muscle coat is visible for approximately seven to eight centimeters, with preserved nerve structures.
